speaker: interviewee
text: [SIDE CONVERSATION]

speaker: interviewer
text: December the 14th, '94; survivor, Krumholz, Lola; Interviewer, Carol Stulberg; Los Angeles, USA; and it's in English. Is the second slate, and it's December the 14th, '94. The survivor is Krumholz, without 't,' Lola Kornfeld; Interviewer, Carol Stulberg; Los Angeles, USA; and it's in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Carol Stulberg. And I have the honor of interviewing Mrs. Lola Krumholz, Wednesday, December 14, 1994, in Los Angeles, California. Mrs. Krumholz, please tell us your name at birth and spell it for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Lola Kornfeld.

speaker: interviewer
text: Spell it, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: K-O-R-N-F-E-L-D.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where and when were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Poland, in Biala-Bielsko.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Biala-- B-I-A-L-A. What do you call that? Not apostrophe, what is that thing? Bielsko-- B-I-E-L-S-K-O.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: January 10, 1917.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the names of your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: David and Hermina Kornfeld.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother's maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Whose name?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother's maiden name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Kornfeld.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And what was the name of your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Isadore Kornfeld.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was four years older than me. I don't remember his birthday.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you just had one brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: One brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your father do to support his family? What did your father do for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father-- my father had a carpet factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe your life to us before the occupation. Tell me about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a happy-go-lucky little girl. [LAUGHS] That's all. I didn't-- I didn't think of anything. I didn't think of anything. I didn't have to go to work. I didn't have to look for a job. I didn't have to-- I didn't have to do anything at all. I went-- I-- I could go up to the factory and help out a little bit sometimes. And sometimes I did not go. Sometimes went-- nothing very hard.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you from a religious family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Not Orthodox. Not Orthodox religion, but religious.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the first signs of trouble? What do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Trouble where? In Poland, or--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, in Poland, always was trouble. But in that time it wasn't so bad. In that time, it was-- first of all, I didn't live in between Poles. I lived in between Austrian people. This belonged, before the war, to Austria. And they didn't Polandize. I went to German schools, too. My brother-- all the schools in German, everybody. Later on, later, later, later on, they started to Polandize the schools. But then I was out of school. But later on, they did it. But they didn't go very far. No, they didn't. They changed one school in Biala, where I was. They changed one school. Because the school where I went when I was a kid was a big, big, big building, a big school. And that was German. And the Poles didn't have big schools, so they took it away in that time. But I was out of school already.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what do you remember about the occupation? What was the first thing you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: About the occupation? I ran away with my mother and with a friend of ours.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stopped in-- we stopped in Cracow. And the other friend went on to Wolow. But we stopped in Cracow with my mother and father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you already met your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we met before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: We met-- [LAUGHS] we met in an organization. And the name of the organization is Betar. This is a Jewish organization. I was-- I was late. I was late. I couldn't make it, because I don't remember why. And I came in, but I came still in in time where I could participate in everything. And, and then we just was like an organization then.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't remember if I met him then, right then, then and there. But I met him maybe a day later. Was it a day later, when I met you? Or the same day? [LAUGHS] Something like that. That happened. And when did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- before the war, right before the war, right before the war broke out, I married. We were-- no! What-- what I say? When did I get married? Richard, when did I get married?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get married in 1942?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when the war broke out, you were already married. And when you came to Cracow, you came with your husband together?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. To Cracow? No, he was in where he was.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the occupation of Poland happened, when the Germans occupied Poland--

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't married then yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened? Where did you go? Tell me what happened--

speaker: interviewee
text: I went with my parents to Cracow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh. And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: And over there we lived in a, a-- first we had family over there. We lived with them. And then we moved to-- we had a, a carpet store over there, where you could live behind the store. So we took this place, and we moved in there, in that place. We heard about, from time to time, from him. But we were not married. I don't remember when I got married. [LAUGHS] And I lived there with my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until-- until one day, he came to Cracow. He did? He came to Cracow one day? Yeah, he did, with his mother. With his mother. And we got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did we got married? Then, there, in Cracow? Or later on in Skala? In Skala.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was a-- this was a-- this was-- it was already talk. In Cracow was already talk that they're going to come and take us all. So we, my parents and and I, went to that place, Skala. This was a mostly a very small place, a very, very small place, where lots of Jews were living. And-- and we settled over there in some kind of a peasant-- peasant-- you rented a room from a peasant. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And this is where we lived. My husband came in that time from wherever he was involved. And then we got married. And we moved to another little house again, a little peasant house. And from then on, we were married. But it was-- we had to go to work over there, because the Germans made you, made you go to work. Because over there, not far from there, was the place [INAUDIBLE] Ojcow. It was a very beautiful vacation place. And they send us there to work over there for the whole day. But-- but you didn't do too much work. You just saw that the day should be over. But the Germans were thinking of something, what to do with the young people. I mean, it was not-- not right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, then we went-- when we-- then came the-- [NON-ENGLISH]. Then came the-- after our wedding-- I don't know what, it's few months after the wedding, something like that, came the-- they took all the Jews. They took them all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Took them where?

speaker: interviewee
text: To kill. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Where was I? Oh, no. Where was I? I forg-- I was with my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no, no. He went first. They took him-- they took all the young men first. Now I remember. So my mother-in-law wanted to go and visit him once. Where he is? What he does? What it is?

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew that he is in the camp, in a-- in a-- in a working camp. So we went. We had a hard time going-- going to Cracow. They stopped us. And they told us, you cannot get in there. They gave us a very, very hard time, the Germans. But we went through. We went through. And next day, we visited him. We were supposed to go back-- he was supposed to go back in a day or two. So I said to my mother-in-law, you know, they give us such a hard time coming here. Let's, let's stay another day or two. In this day or two, they took all the, all the Jewish families. Everyone. Everyone, the whole thing, from that place. And they send it to the death camps. But we didn't know in that time that that is a death camp. And we didn't know at that time that they kill Jews. And we didn't know in that time where the people are going. And in that time, they took my mother and my father, too. I didn't know. We didn't know nothing. Nobody knew nothing. I had a cousin over there. And she had a woman, a Catholic woman. And she sent that woman to follow the train where they are going. Well, she came back, and she said that the train goes through a certain-- before Lublin. And everybody has to get out from the plane, and you never hear nothing anymore from the people. And nobody could go any farther. This is what she-- she told me. Because her parents went too. But in that time, we didn't know that, that they kill-- that they are taking Jews to be killed. We just thought they take them to another camp, or to another, I don't know what. We didn't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we had so much on our head to stay alive that we didn't-- we didn't make-- we didn't think nothing. [SIGHS] That's how it-- that's how it was until that-- that point. I don't-- I don't know what was after that. What was after that? After that, we, we, we went to Cracow? Yeah, well, we couldn't go anymore and live in Skala, because we didn't have work to live in Skala. They didn't let us in anymore. So we went back to Cracow.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Cracow, we went to live with his-- with his-- before the war, there was a woman who worked in their store in [POLISH]. And she and her-- her husband was a shoemaker, what made all the boots for the, for the, for the Germans. So she took us in. She really didn't have what to take us in. She had one room and a kitchen. This-- there was really no space, but she took us in. And we stayed-- we stayed there. But to stay there, we had to have a work assignment. We had to be assigned that you work. And he went to the, to his man, what he makes the boots for them. And they gave him the work assignment for us. That was a work assignment to Schindler. But nobody knew nothing in that time. And we got the work assignment. And we went, we were the first seven Jews in Schindler. There was nobody there. And Schindler was not known. Nobody knew him. And we started to work there. And we went every morning to-- to work, and then we came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the beginning, I was-- I was taking the thing with the wet pots and pans and put it in the drying. They had such very long drying-- I don't know what you call it. And it is very, very hot. It was very, very hot in there. And then later on, my husband taught me how to, how to, how to weld, how to put the pots, how to put the handles on the pots and pans. And I was, I was, I was doing it all the time. Then came a day-- then came a day where Schindler came to the factory, and he took-- and he told us, tonight you don't go home. My shift was over. I didn't have to work no more.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't go. You give me just your IDs and stay there. There was a little come-- what do you call? Room?

speaker: interviewee
text: To go in there and sit there. In the morning, he came back. And he brought us the IDs back, with a-- with a stamp, that we can go back, we can go back into the, into the ghetto. And we went back to the ghetto. We came to the ghetto. The ghetto was empty. We didn't know what happened. Then we went, we went where we lived before, and they told us what happened. His mother was there. And the woman what we were staying was, and the husband was there. Everybody was there, but the ghetto was empty. It was empty. We didn't know what happened. But we found out. When the news spread that Schindler rescued his people, everybody wanted to come to Schindler. But he couldn't take that many people. But later on, he built another additional factory in the back of this factory, of the [? enamelware ?] factory. He built a factory, something what they made for the, for the German defense. Something what they made for the German-- the Germans needed. And he got a OK to build this factory. And now he, he hired more people. And he hired Polish people and more Jewish people. In that-- we were only seven, seven of us, so it was more right away. But they didn't let-- but we went, from the beginning, we went from the ghetto every morning to the factory and back. But they didn't allow that. So Schindler built a little camp behind, behind his factory for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we lived in these camps. We lived in these camps, and there was another factory not very far away from us. What was the name of it? KFI-- no, F-- something like abbreviation. And these people were allowed to, to come in and sleep.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- but we didn't know, we didn't know nothing else. We didn't understand what was going on. We didn't understand what it was. But in the meantime, the war [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] came. We didn't listen, no radio, no television, nothing. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, one day, it was, it was-- what was it? They took the-- they took the men, my husband. And all the other men what lived there with us. And many times, I mentioned him to my husband. Is all these men what was with us, another life. I-- I mention it very often to him.

speaker: interviewee
text: One couple is-- one couple is in-- in New York, they live. She was with me at the time. And he was with me. He was, he was there in the, in the factory. And then he went with them. But the other ones wasn't-- nobody left. You know, the-- they had to evacuate Plaszow, Krakau-Plaszow, because the Russians came closer, and closer, and closer.

speaker: interviewer
text: But so they had to evacuate. They wanted to kill, kill us all. So they didn't know how to kill us all. So they-- what did they do? First of all, they closed the camp next to Schindler. And we all had to go upstairs.

speaker: interviewee
text: In that time, they made the list. That was the time when they made the list. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And that list-- who had money, or who had, or who had acquaintances-- [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry. Stop it.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's OK. That's OK. Nothing happened. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: You're rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, Mrs. Krumholz, excuse me. You were starting to tell us about the list that Schindler made.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well then, but we didn't know anything, that a list is going on. Because they wanted-- everybody wanted to-- to live. Everybody wanted to rescue themselves. And everybody on there-- and they knew then, when this has to do with Schindler, you will live.

speaker: interviewer
text: So every-- so what, what did they do? They took the, the men what was there, and had some money to give this one man. Marcel Goldberg was his name. So, and he couldn't put them on the list. So what he did, he took out the men what was there before, and he put them in. He put them in. Schindler came one day, and he said, where are my people? So they told him, the women are only left. The men are all gone. Well, he said, you're not going to do with the women what you did with the men. I went all my women. All my women what was with me, I want.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is how we got all rescued. Because if you wouldn't get rescued, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I wouldn't have lived, and none of, none of that transport would have lived. We went, we went, we went to-- we were taken out from them-- from the transport, it all. I remember we were standing there outside, and they were reading the list. And in that time, I ran for my mother-in-law. Because a day or two, and they took her out from, from, from, from the list and from the barracks because she was too old. So when they started reading the list, I ran for my mother-in-law to bring her over.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know exactly what was going to happen, because nobody knew nothing. But when they were reading the list, I ran for her. I ran, and I brought her. And the minute I came back, they were reading-- what is her-- what was her name? Whatever her-- what was her name? Szenker.

speaker: interviewee
text: She had another name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Szenker.

speaker: interviewee
text: Szenker, Rivka Szenker. They were reading, 'Rivka Szenker, here?' Here. 'Lola Krumholz, here?' Here. That's all I wanted. And we went all with the train to Brunnlitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now at this point, you became separated from your husband? Is that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband went already a  day, a day or two before. I didn't even know where he went or what he went. I have never seen him anymore. So I-- I didn't know nothing. And we went to, to, to, to Brunnlitz. We came-- when we came to Brunnlitz, the men were already there. The men what came into the list, and so-- not very many are here today. It's a shame to think about it. And I think about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we stayed in Schindler's. We were in Schindler's. We stayed in Schindler's. We had-- he had-- he didn't have any barracks. Only he had-- there was a factory. And there was a big loft, and another big loft. And that's so how, that's how we stayed.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to work. There was no work done. There was no work done, because there was no more work. The rations already, took already everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: One day we got very scared, because that, that, that murderer that-- what is his name? Goeth? Goeth? What is his name? Goeth-- came to Schindler for a visit. But he couldn't wear anymore his belt. He couldn't wear anymore anything. But, but he-- Are you talking about the commandant of the concentration camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Amon Goeth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Goeth, yeah. Amon Goeth, yeah. So he came. We were so scared. We didn't know what he wanted.

speaker: interviewee
text: So Schindler told us all, nothing will happen here. I told him already, you're not allowed to touch anything and nobody. So he was, and he looked around, and looked around, and looked around. And he went because he couldn't do anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, he was already on the run. He was running already, because he didn't have where to go. He didn't have-- [INAUDIBLE] I want to tell you something. I went with my husband to the movies in, in, in, in Czechoslovakia. And all of a sudden on the news reel came Goeth. Goeth! I yelled out, look! Look! Goeth! I said. I yelled out, because I, I was, you know. But he went-- he was killed in Poland. Him and few Jews were killed in Poland, on the-- on the marketplace in Cracow. They all hung him, hung him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the living conditions while you were working for Schindler at Brunnlitz. What was it like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- in the factories was the hard, is hard work. In the factory was hard work, 12 hours a day, or 12 hours at night. We worked very hard. But you know, thank god I was young in that time. And I could do it. A older person couldn't have done it. And, and we had food enough. Always, food enough, because the Poles worked with us. And if you gave them something, they brought you food. So we had always-- we were not hungry. Well, this was one good thing. We were not hungry at all. Until, until-- we were in Schindler's-- until the order came to go to Brunnlitz. We were in Schindler's then. And then in Brunnlitz was a different story. We were-- we, we worked, we were supposed to work in the-- I don't know what they had it for, to the, to the, to the planes, or to the trains, or to the-- I don't know, anything. And they said, he said, he brainwashed them that its so and so many go out daily. And so and so many go out this way. And he brain-- I don't know how he, how did, but-- and he took in very many Jews next to the, to the other part, what he built over there. And this is how, how, how it was.

speaker: interviewee
text: And all of a sudden, we didn't know that the war is over. And we didn't know that there was coming over, coming to be over in few days or so. Because we only, we only heard about something that he took few, few men out from there, and he, and he dressed them up. And they took a, they took something to open up a train. You know the trains what came? Not the train what you sit there, only-- Transport.

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: Cattle cars. Cattle cars.

speaker: interviewee
text: Cattle car. But we didn't know what for. All of a sudden, we saw a bunch of people coming all frozen, all hungry, frozen. And they looked like, like they will, they will, they will not live. So they went to another barrack over there. But they lived! They lived. They were-- they lived until we were liberated. But then, I didn't know what might happen. I asked Page what happened to them. And Page told me that they live.
